<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT &lt;columns&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM &lt;tbl&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE &lt;predicate&gt; ;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Once again, let’s consider our table Person(<code>name</code>, <code>age</code>, <code>num_dogs</code>). Suppose we want to see how many dogs each person owns — same as before — but this time we only care about the dog-owners who are adults. Let’s walk through this SQL query:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT name, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE age &gt;= 18 ;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>When reasoning about query execution you can use the following rule: each clause in a SQL query happens in the order it’s written, except for <code>SELECT</code> which happens last. This is not necessarily the order the database actually does these operations (more on this later in the semester), but it is easy to think about and will always give us the correct answer. The <code>FROM</code> clause tells us we’re interested in the Person table, so this is the table we start with:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ben</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">7</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">2</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Next we move on to the <code>WHERE</code> clause. It tells us that we only want to keep the rows satisfying the predicate age &gt;= 18, so we remove the row with Ben, and are left with the following table:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>And finally, we <code>SELECT</code> the columns <code>name</code> and <code>num_dogs</code> to obtain our final result. (Again, any permutation of this result is equally valid so you shouldn’t make any assumptions about the order of the rows.) This gives us our final table:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<h2 id="boolean-operators" class="heading-element"><span>Boolean operators</span>
 <a href="#boolean-operators" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>If you want to filter on more complicated predicates, you can use the boolean operators <code>NOT</code>, <code>AND</code>, and <code>OR</code>. For instance, if we only cared about dog-owners who are not only adults, but also own more than 3 dogs, then we would write the following query:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT name, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE age &gt;= 18
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">AND num_dogs &gt; 3;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>As in Python, this is the order of evaluation for boolean operators:</p>
<ol>
<li><code>NOT</code></li>
<li><code>AND</code></li>
<li><code>OR</code></li>
</ol>
<p>That said, it is good practice to avoid ambiguity by adding parentheses even when they are not strictly necessary.</p>
<h2 id="filtering-null-values" class="heading-element"><span>Filtering Null Values</span>
 <a href="#filtering-null-values" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>In SQL, there is a special value called NULL, which can be used as a value for any data type, and represents an “unknown” or “missing” value.</p>
<p>Bear in mind that some values in your database may be <code>NULL</code> whether you like it or not, so it’s good to know how SQL handles them. It pretty much boils down to the following rules:</p>
<ul>
<li>
<p>If you do anything with <code>NULL</code>, you’ll just get <code>NULL</code>. For instance if x is <code>NULL</code>, then x &gt; 3, 1 = x, and x + 4 all evaluate to <code>NULL</code>. Even x = <code>NULL</code> would evaluate to <code>NULL</code>; if you want to check whether x is <code>NULL</code>, then write x IS <code>NULL</code> or x IS NOT <code>NULL</code> instead.</p>
</li>
<li>
<p><code>NULL</code> is falsey, meaning that <code>WHERE NULL</code> is just like <code>WHERE FALSE</code>. The row in question does not get included.</p>
</li>
<li>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-plaintext" data-lang="plaintext"><span class="line"><span class="cl">NULL</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>short-circuits with boolean operators. That means a boolean expression involving</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-plaintext" data-lang="plaintext"><span class="line"><span class="cl">NULL</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>will evaluate to:</p>
<ul>
<li><code>TRUE</code>, if it’d evaluate to <code>TRUE</code> regardless of whether the NULL value is really <code>TRUE</code> or <code>FALSE</code>.</li>
<li><code>FALSE</code>, if it’d evaluate to <code>FALSE</code> regardless of whether the <code>NULL</code> value is really <code>TRUE</code> or <code>FALSE</code>.</li>
<li>Or <code>NULL</code>, if it depends on the <code>NULL</code> value.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<p>Let’s walk through this query as an example:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT name, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE age &lt;= 20
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">OR num_dogs = 3;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Let’s assume we change some values to NULL, so after evaluating the FROM clause we are left with:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ben</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Next we move on to the <code>WHERE</code> clause. It tells us that we only want to keep the rows satisfying the predicate <code>age</code> &lt;= 20 OR <code>num_dogs</code> = 3. Let’s consider each row one at a time:</p>
<ul>
<li>For Ace, <code>age</code> &lt;= 20 evaluates to <code>TRUE</code> so the claim is satisfied.</li>
<li>For Ada, <code>age</code> &lt;= 20 evaluates to <code>NULL</code> but <code>num_dogs</code> = 3 evaluates to <code>TRUE</code> so the claim is satisfied.</li>
<li>For Ben, <code>age</code> &lt;= 20 evaluates to <code>NULL</code> and <code>num_dogs</code> = 3 evaluates to <code>NULL</code> so the overall expression is <code>NULL</code> which has a falsey value.</li>
<li>For Cho, <code>age</code> &lt;= 20 evaluates to <code>FALSE</code> and <code>num_dogs</code> = 3 evaluates to <code>NULL</code> so the overall expression evaluates to <code>NULL</code> (because it depends on the value of the <code>NULL</code>). Because <code>NULL</code> is falsey, this row will be excluded.</li>
</ul>
<p>Thus we keep only Ace and Ada.</p>
<h2 id="grouping-and-aggregation" class="heading-element"><span>Grouping and Aggregation</span>
 <a href="#grouping-and-aggregation" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>When you’re working with a very large database, it is useful to be able to summarize your data so that you can better understand the general trends at play. Let’s see how.</p>
<h3 id="summarizing-columns-of-data" class="heading-element"><span>Summarizing columns of data</span>
 <a href="#summarizing-columns-of-data" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h3><p>With SQL you are able to summarize entire columns of data using built-in aggregate functions. The most common ones are <code>SUM</code>, <code>AVG</code>, <code>MAX</code>, <code>MIN</code>, and <code>COUNT</code>. Here are some important characteristics of aggregate functions:</p>
<ul>
<li>The input to an aggregate function is the name of a column, and the output is a single value that summarizes all the data within that column.</li>
<li>Every aggregate ignores NULL values except for <code>COUNT(*)</code>. (So <code>COUNT(&lt;column&gt;)</code> returns the number of non-NULL values in the specified column, whereas <code>COUNT(*)</code> returns the number of rows in the table overall.)</li>
</ul>
<p>For example, consider this variant of our table <code>People(name, age, num_dogs)</code> from earlier, where we are now unsure how many dogs Ben owns:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ben</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">7</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>With this table in mind …</p>
<ul>
<li><code>SUM(age)</code> is 72.0 and <code>SUM(num_dogs)</code> is 10.0.</li>
<li><code>AVG(age)</code> is 18.0 and <code>AVG(num_dogs)</code> is 3.3333333333333333.</li>
<li><code>MAX(age)</code> is 27, and <code>MAX(num_dogs)</code> is 4.</li>
<li><code>MIN(age)</code> is 7, and <code>MIN(num_dogs)</code> is 3.</li>
<li><code>COUNT(age)</code> is 4, <code>COUNT(num_dogs)</code> is 3, and <code>COUNT(*)</code> is 4.</li>
</ul>
<p>So, if we desired the range of ages represented in our database, then we could use the query below and it would produce the result 20. (Technically it would produce a one-by-one table containing the number 20, but SQL treats it the same as the number 20 itself.)</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT MAX(age) - MIN(age)
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Or, if we desired the average number of dogs owned by adults, then we could write this:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT AVG(num_dogs)
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE age &gt;= 18;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><h3 id="summarizing-groups-of-data" class="heading-element"><span>Summarizing Groups of Data</span>
 <a href="#summarizing-groups-of-data" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h3><p>Now you know how to summarize an entire column of your database into a single number. More often than not, though, we want a little finer granularity than that. This is possible with the <code>GROUP BY</code> clause, which allows us to split our data into groups and then summarize each group separately. Here’s the syntax:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT &lt;columns&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM &lt;tbl&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE &lt;predicate&gt; -- Filter out rows (before grouping).
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">GROUP BY &lt;columns&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">HAVING &lt;predicate&gt;; -- Filter out groups (after grouping).</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Notice we also have a brand new <code>HAVING</code> clause, which is actually very similar to <code>WHERE</code>. The difference?</p>
<ul>
<li><code>WHERE</code> occurs <em>before grouping</em>. It filters out uninteresting <em>rows</em>.</li>
<li><code>HAVING</code> occurs <em>after grouping</em>. It filters out uninteresting <em>groups</em>.</li>
</ul>
<p>To explore all these new mechanics let’s see another step-by-step example. This time our query will find the average number of dogs owned, for each adult age represented in our database. We will exclude any age for which we only have one datum.</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT age, AVG(num_dogs)
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE age &gt;= 18
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">GROUP BY age
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">HAVING COUNT(*) &gt; 1;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Let us assume the <code>Person</code> table is now:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ben</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">7</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">2</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ema</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">2</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ian</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Jay</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">5</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Mae</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">33</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">8</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Rex</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">1</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Next we move on to the <code>WHERE</code> clause. It tells us that we only want to keep the rows satisfying the predicate <code>age &gt;= 18</code>, so we remove the row with Ben.</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ema</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">2</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ian</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Jay</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">5</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Mae</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">33</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">8</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Rex</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">1</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Now for the interesting part. We arrive at the <code>GROUP BY</code> clause, which tells us to categorize the data by age. We end up with a group of all the adults 20 years old, a group of all the adults 18 years old, a group of all the adults 27 years old, and a group of all the adults 33 years old. You can now think of the table like this:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace Ema Ian</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20 20 20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4 2 3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada Jay</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18 18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3 5</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho Rex</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27 27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3 1</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Mae</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">33</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">8</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>The <code>HAVING</code> clause tells us we only want to keep the groups satisfying the predicate <code>COUNT(*) &gt; 1</code> — that is, the groups that contain more than one row. We discard the group that contains only Mae.</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace Ema Ian</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20 20 20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4 2 3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada Jay</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18 18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3 5</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho Rex</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27 27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3 1</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Last but not least, every group gets collapsed into a single row. According to our <code>SELECT</code> clause, each such row must contain two things:</p>
<ul>
<li>The <code>age</code> corresponding to the group.</li>
<li>The <code>AVG(num_dogs)</code> for the group.</li>
</ul>
<p>Our final result looks like this:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">AVG(num_dogs)</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3.0</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4.0</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">2.0</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>So, to recap, here’s how you should go about a query that follows the template above:</p>
<ul>
<li>Start with the table specified in the <code>FROM</code> clause.</li>
<li>Filter out uninteresting rows, keeping only the ones that satisfy the <code>WHERE</code> clause.</li>
<li>Put data into groups, according to the <code>GROUP BY</code> clause.</li>
<li>Filter out uninteresting groups, keeping only the ones that satisfy the <code>HAVING</code> clause.</li>
<li>Collapse each group into a single row, containing the fields specified in the <code>SELECT</code> clause.</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="a-word-of-caution" class="heading-element"><span>A Word of Caution</span>
 <a href="#a-word-of-caution" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>So that’s how grouping and aggregation work, but before we move on we must emphasize one last thing regarding illegal queries. We’ll start by considering these two examples:</p>
<ol>
<li>Though it’s not immediately obvious, this query actually produces an error:</li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT age, AVG(num_dogs)
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>What’s the issue? <code>age</code> is an entire column of numbers, whereas <code>AVG(num_dogs)</code> is just a single number. This is problematic because a properly formed table must have the same amount of rows in each column.</p>
<ol>
<li>This query does not work either, for a very similar reason:</li>
</ol>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT age, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">GROUP BY age;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>After grouping by age we obtain a table like this:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">age</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num_dogs</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ace Ema Ian</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">20 20 20</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">4 2 3</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Ada Jay</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">18 18</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3 5</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Cho Rex</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">27 27</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">3 1</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">Mae</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">33</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">8</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Then the <code>SELECT</code> clause’s job is to collapse each group into a single row. Each such row must contain two things:</p>
<ul>
<li>The <code>age</code> corresponding to the group, which is a single number.</li>
<li>The <code>num_dogs</code> for the group, which is an entire column of numbers.</li>
</ul>
<p>The takeaway from all this? If you’re going to do any grouping / aggregation at all, then you must only <code>SELECT</code> grouped / aggregated columns.</p>
<h2 id="order-by" class="heading-element"><span>Order By</span>
 <a href="#order-by" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Before, we mentioned that the ordering of the output rows was usually nondeterministic in SQL. If you want the rows of your table to appear in a certain order you must use an <code>ORDER BY</code> clause.</p>
<p>Here is an example query using an <code>ORDER BY</code> clause:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT name, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ORDER BY num_dogs, name;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>You can include as many columns as you want in the ORDER BY clause. We first sort on the first column listed, and then break any ties with the second column listed, and then break any remaining ties with the third column listed, and so on. By default, the sort order is ascending, but if we want the order in descending order we add the DESC keyword after the column name. If we wanted to sort by the num_dogs ascending and break ties by name descending, we would use the following query:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT name, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ORDER BY num_dogs, name DESC;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><h2 id="limit" class="heading-element"><span>Limit</span>
 <a href="#limit" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Sometimes we only want to see a few rows in our table, even if more rows match all of our other conditions. To do this we can add a <code>LIMIT</code> clause to the end of our query to cap the number of rows that will be returned. Note: the same rows may not always be returned by queries using <code>LIMIT</code> if an <code>ORDER BY</code> is not used or if there are ties in the ordering. Here is a query that only returns one row:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT name, num_dogs
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM Person
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">LIMIT 1;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><h2 id="logical-processing-order" class="heading-element"><span>Logical Processing Order</span>
 <a href="#logical-processing-order" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>SQL Queries are typically processed in a different order than they’re written. Here’s the order- try to develop an intuition as to why this order would make more sense to a machine than how queries are usually written:</p>
<ol>
<li><code>FROM</code> (find the table that is being referenced, join if needed)</li>
<li><code>WHERE</code> (filters out rows)</li>
<li><code>GROUP BY</code> (aggregate)</li>
<li><code>HAVING</code> (filters out groups)</li>
<li><code>SELECT</code> (choose columns)</li>
<li><code>ORDER BY</code> (sort)</li>
<li><code>LIMIT</code> (cut off the output)</li>
</ol>
<h3 id="a-note-on-aliasing" class="heading-element"><span>A note on aliasing</span>
 <a href="#a-note-on-aliasing" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h3><p>One consequence of Logical Processing Order is that <strong>we cannot use aliases in WHERE, GROUP BY, or HAVING</strong> because they are processed before any alias is defined in SELECT!</p>
<p>For example, <code>SELECT name as clubname WHERE clubname = 'Open Computing Facility'</code> is NOT a valid query in standard SQL.</p>
<p>However, since ORDER BY and LIMIT come afterwards, we are allowed to use aliases there.</p>
<h2 id="conclusion" class="heading-element"><span>Conclusion</span>
 <a href="#conclusion" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Congratulations - we have covered a lot of SQL! To help remember the ordering of the SQL stages, here is the syntax of a query involving the expressions we have learned so far:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span><span class="lnt">6
</span><span class="lnt">7
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT &lt;columns&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM &lt;tbl&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE &lt;predicate&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">GROUP BY &lt;columns&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">HAVING &lt;predicate&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ORDER BY &lt;columns&gt;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">LIMIT &lt;num&gt;;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><h1 id="sql-part-2---joins--subqueries" class="heading-element"><span>SQL Part 2 - Joins &amp; Subqueries</span>
 <a href="#sql-part-2---joins--subqueries" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h1><h2 id="cross-join" class="heading-element"><span>Cross Join</span>
 <a href="#cross-join" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>The simplest join is called <strong>cross join</strong>, which is also known as a cross product or a cartesian product. A cross join is the result of combining every row from the left table with every row from the right table. To do a cross join, simply comma separate the tables you would like to join. Here is an example:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses, enrollment;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>If the courses table looked like this:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS189</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">ML</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>And the enrollment table looked like this:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">c_num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>The result of the query would be:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">c_num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS189</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">ML</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS189</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">ML</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>The cartesian product often contains much more information than we are actually interested in. Let’s say we wanted all of the information about a course (num, name, and num of students enrolled in it). We cannot just blindly join every row from the left table with every row from the right table. There are rows that have two different courses in them! To account for this we will add a <strong>join condition</strong> in the <code>WHERE</code> clause to ensure that each row is only about one class.</p>
<p>To get the enrollment information for a course properly we want to make sure that <code>num</code> in the courses table is equal to <code>c_num</code> in the enrollment table because they are the same thing. The correct query is:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses, enrollment;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE num = c_num</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>which produces:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">c_num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Notice that CS189, which was present in the <code>courses</code> table but not in the <code>enrollment</code> table, is not included. Since it does not appear as a <code>c_num</code> value in enrollment, it cannot fulfill the join condition <code>num = c_num</code>.</p>
<p>If we really want CS189 to appear anyway, there are ways to do this that we will discuss later.</p>
<h2 id="inner-join" class="heading-element"><span>Inner Join</span>
 <a href="#inner-join" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>The cross join works great, but it seems a little sloppy. We are including join logic in the <code>WHERE</code> clause. It can be difficult to find what the join condition is. In contrast, the <strong>inner join</strong> allows you to specify the condition in the <code>ON</code> clause. Here is the syntax:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT column_name(s)
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM table1
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">INNER JOIN table2
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON table1_column_name = table2_column_name;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>The <code>table1_column_name = table2_column_name</code> is the join condition. Let’s write the query that gets us all of the course information as an inner join:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses INNER JOIN enrollment
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON num = c_num ;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>This query is logically the exact same query as the query we ran in the previous section. The inner join is essentially syntatic sugar for a cross join with a join condition in the <code>WHERE</code> clause like we demonstrated before.</p>
<h2 id="outer-joins" class="heading-element"><span>Outer Joins</span>
 <a href="#outer-joins" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Now let’s address the problem we encountered before when we left out CS189 because it did not have any enrollment information. This situation happens frequently. We still want to keep all the data from a relation even if it does not have a “match” in the table we are joining it with. To fix this problem we will use a <strong>left outer join</strong>. The left outer join makes sure that every row from the left table will appear in the output. If a row does not have any matches with the right table, the row is still included and the columns from the right table are filled in with <code>NULL</code>. Let’s fix our query:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses LEFT OUTER JOIN enrollment
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON num = c_num;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>This will produce the following output:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">c_num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS189</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">ML</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Notice that CS189 is now included and the columns that should be from the right table <code>(c_num, students)</code> are <code>NULL</code>.</p>
<p>The <strong>right outer join</strong> is the exact same thing as the left outer join but it keeps all the rows from the right table instead of the left table. The following query is identical to the query above that uses the left outer join:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM enrollment RIGHT OUTER JOIN courses
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON num = c_num;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Notice that I flipped the order of the joins and changed <code>LEFT</code> to <code>RIGHT</code> because now courses is on the right side.</p>
<p>Let’s say we add a row to our enrollment table now:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">c_num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS160</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">400</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>But we still want to present all of the information that we have. If we just use a left or a right join we have to pick between using all of the information in the left table or all of the information in the right table. With what we know so far, it is impossible for us to include the information that we have about <em>both</em> CS189 and CS160 because they occur in different tables and do not have matches in the other table. To fix this we can use the <strong>full outer join</strong> which guarantees that all rows from each table will appear in the output. If a row from either table does not have a match it will still show up in the output and the columns from the other table in the join will be <code>NULL</code>.</p>
<p>To include all of data we have let’s change the query to be:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses FULL OUTER JOIN enrollment
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON num = c_num;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>which produces the following output:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">c_num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS189</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">ML</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">NULL</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS160</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">400</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<h2 id="name-conflicts" class="heading-element"><span>Name Conflicts</span>
 <a href="#name-conflicts" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Up to this point our tables have had columns with different names. But what happens if we change the enrollment table so that it’s <code>c_num</code> column is now called <code>num</code>?</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS160</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">400</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Now there is a <code>num</code> column in both tables, so simply using <code>num</code> in your query is ambiguous. We now have to specify which table’s column we are referring to. To do this, we put the table name and a period in front of the column name. Here is an example of doing an inner join of the two tables now:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses INNER JOIN enrollment
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON courses.num = enrollment.num</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>The result is:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>It can be annoying to type out the entire table name each time we refer to it, so instead we can <strong>alias</strong> the table name. This allows us to rename the table for the rest of the query as something else (usually only a few characters). To do this, after listing the table in the <code>FROM</code> we add <code>AS &lt;alias name&gt;</code>. Here is an equivalent query that uses aliases:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses AS c INNER JOIN enrollment AS e
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON c.num = e.num;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>The result is:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Aliases can also be used in the SELECT clause to rename the column names of the output. If we execute the following query:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT c.num AS num1, c.name, e.num AS num2, e.students
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses AS c INNER JOIN enrollment AS e
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">ON c.num = e.num;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>The output will be:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num1</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">num2</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">700</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<h2 id="natural-join" class="heading-element"><span>Natural Join</span>
 <a href="#natural-join" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Often in relational databases, the columns you want to join on will have the same name. To make it easier to write queries, SQL has the <strong>natural join</strong> which automatically does an equijoin (equijoin <code>=</code> checks if columns are equivalent) on columns with the same name in different tables. The following query is the same as explicitly doing an inner join on the <code>num</code> columns in each table:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courses NATURAL JOIN enrollment;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>The join condition: <code>courses.num = enrollment.num</code> is implicit. While this is convenient, natural joins are not often used in practice because they are confusing to read and because adding columns that are not related to the query can change the output.</p>
<h2 id="subqueries" class="heading-element"><span>Subqueries</span>
 <a href="#subqueries" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Subqueries allow you to write more powerful queries. Let’s look at an example…</p>
<p>Let’s say you want to find the course num of every course that has a higher than average num of students. You cannot include an aggregation expression (like AVG) in the WHERE clause because aggregation happens after rows have been filtered. This may seem challenging at first, but sub-queries make it easy:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span><span class="lnt">6
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT num
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM enrollment;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE students &gt;= (
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> SELECT AVG(students)
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> FROM enrollment;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">);</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>The output of this query is:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>The inner subquery calculated the average and returned one row. The outer query compared the <code>students</code> value for each row to what the subquery returned to determine if the row should be kept. Note that this query would be invalid if the subquery returned more than one row because <code>&gt;=</code> is meaningless for more than one number. If it returned more than one row we would have to use a set operator like <code>ALL</code>.</p>
<h2 id="correlated-subqueries" class="heading-element"><span>Correlated Subqueries</span>
 <a href="#correlated-subqueries" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>The subquery can also be correlated with the outer query. Each row essentially gets plugged in to the subquery and then the subquery uses the values of that row. To illustrate this point, let’s write a query that returns all of the classes that appear in both tables.</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span><span class="lnt">6
</span><span class="lnt">7
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM classes
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE EXISTS (
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> FROM enrollment
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> WHERE classes.num = enrollment.num
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">);</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>As expected, this query returns:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 </tr>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">DB</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>Let’s start by examining the subquery. It compares the <code>classes.num</code> (the num of the class from the current row) to every <code>enrollment.num</code> and returns the row if they match. Therefore, the only rows that will ever be returned are rows with classes that occur in each table. The <code>EXISTS</code> keyword is a set operator that returns true if any rows are returned by the subquery and false if otherwise. For <code>CS186</code> and <code>CS188</code> it will return true (because a row is returned by the subquery), but for <code>CS189</code> it will return false. There are a lot of other set operators you should know (including <code>ANY</code>, <code>ALL</code>, <code>UNION</code>, <code>INTERSECT</code>, <code>DIFFERENCE</code>, <code>IN</code>) but we will not cover any others in this note (there is plenty of documentation for these operators online).</p>
<h2 id="subqueries-in-the-from" class="heading-element"><span>Subqueries in the From</span>
 <a href="#subqueries-in-the-from" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>You can also use subqueries in the <code>FROM</code> clause. This lets you create a temporary table to query from. Here is an example:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span><span class="lnt">6
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT *
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM (
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> SELECT num
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> FROM classes
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">) AS a
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE num = &#39;CS186&#39;;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Returns:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS186</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>The subquery returns only the <code>num</code> column of the original table, so only the <code>num</code> column will appear in the output. One thing to note is that subqueries in the <code>FROM</code> cannot usually be correlated with other tables listed in the <code>FROM</code>. There is a work around for this, but it is out of scope for this course. A cleaner way of doing this is using common table expressions (or views if you want to reuse the temporary table in other queries) but we will not cover this in the note.</p>
<h2 id="subquery-factoring" class="heading-element"><span>Subquery Factoring</span>
 <a href="#subquery-factoring" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>Subquery factoring can simplify queries by giving a subquery a name to be used later. To do this, we use the <code>WITH</code> clause:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span><span class="lnt">4
</span><span class="lnt">5
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">WITH courseEnrollment AS (
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> SELECT c.num AS num1, c.name, e.num AS num2, e.students
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> FROM courses AS c INNER JOIN enrollment AS e
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl"> ON c.num = e.num;
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">)</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>We can then treat <code>courseEnrollment</code> as if it were its own table in a future query:</p>
<div class="highlight"><div class="chroma">
<table class="lntable"><tr><td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code><span class="lnt">1
</span><span class="lnt">2
</span><span class="lnt">3
</span></code></pre></td>
<td class="lntd">
<pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"><code class="language-fallback" data-lang="fallback"><span class="line"><span class="cl">SELECT num1, name, students
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">FROM courseEnrollment
</span></span><span class="line"><span class="cl">WHERE students &gt; 700;</span></span></code></pre></td></tr></table>
</div>
</div><p>Returns:</p>
<table>
 <thead>
 <tr>
 <th style="text-align: left">num1</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">name</th>
 <th style="text-align: left">students</th>
 </tr>
 </thead>
 <tbody>
 <tr>
 <td style="text-align: left">CS188</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">AI</td>
 <td style="text-align: left">800</td>
 </tr>
 </tbody>
</table>
<p>The subquery returns only the <code>num</code> column of the original table, so only the <code>num</code> column will appear in the output. One thing to note is that subqueries in the <code>FROM</code> cannot usually be correlated with other tables listed in the <code>FROM</code>. There is a work around for this, but it is out of scope for this course. A cleaner way of doing this is using common table expressions (or views if you want to reuse the temporary table in other queries) but we will not cover this in the note.</p>

item.title: Computer Networking
item.description:
<p>《计算机网络-自顶向下方法》笔记</p>
<p>计算机网络课堂笔记，参考用书《计算机网络-自顶向下方法》。</p>
<h1 id="计算机网络和因特网" class="heading-element"><span>计算机网络和因特网</span>
 <a href="#%e8%ae%a1%e7%ae%97%e6%9c%ba%e7%bd%91%e7%bb%9c%e5%92%8c%e5%9b%a0%e7%89%b9%e7%bd%91" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h1><h2 id="计算机网络" class="heading-element"><span>计算机网络</span>
 <a href="#%e8%ae%a1%e7%ae%97%e6%9c%ba%e7%bd%91%e7%bb%9c" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h2><p>起源是为了形成一个自主、自治、互联的计算机通信系统。</p>
<h3 id="构成描述" class="heading-element"><span>构成描述</span>
 <a href="#%e6%9e%84%e6%88%90%e6%8f%8f%e8%bf%b0" class="heading-mark">
 <svg class="octicon octicon-link" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="m7.775 3.275 1.25-1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1 4.95 4.95l-2.5 2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1-4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1 .018-1.042.751.751 0 0 1 1.042-.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l2.5-2.5a2.002 2.002 0 0 0-2.83-2.83l-1.25 1.25a.751.751 0 0 1-1.042-.018.751.751 0 0 1-.018-1.042Zm-4.69 9.64a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 2.83 0l1.25-1.25a.751.751 0 0 1 1.042.018.751.751 0 0 1 .018 1.042l-1.25 1.25a3.5 3.5 0 1 1-4.95-4.95l2.5-2.5a3.5 3.5 0 0 1 4.95 0 .751.751 0 0 1-.018 1.042.751.751 0 0 1-1.042.018 1.998 1.998 0 0 0-2.83 0l-2.5 2.5a1.998 1.998 0 0 0 0 2.83Z"></path></svg>
 </a>
</h3><p>因特网也被称为“网中网”，我们所用的网络都是大网中的小网（套娃）。</p>
<ul>
<li>
<p>接入网络的设备——网络边缘</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>主机</strong>（host） = <strong>端系统</strong>（end system）</li>
<li>运行的联网app</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li>
<p>通信链路</p>
<p>（communication link）——接入网</p>
<ul>
<li>媒介：同轴电缆、铜线、光纤、无线电频谱</li>
<li>传输速率：带宽 bps</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li>
<p>分组交换机</p>
<p>（packet switch）——网络核心</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>路由器</strong>（router）和 <strong>链路层交换机</strong>（link-layer switch）</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li>
<p><strong>协议</strong>：控制发送、接收信息。比如，TCP, IP, HTTP等等。每个层次有每个层次的协议。</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>网络标准</p>
<ul>
<li>因特网工程任务组（<strong>IETF</strong>）： 制定网络标准的组织</li>
<li>请求评论（<strong>RFC</strong>）：IETF的标准文档</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<p><img loading="lazy" src="https://i.loli.net/2020/03/19/J1qlUrQx9LiTFY2.png" alt="img" srcset="https://i.loli.net/2020/03/19/J1qlUrQx9LiTFY2.png?size=small, https://i.loli.net/2020/03/19/J1qlUrQx9LiTFY2.png?size=medium 1.5x, https://i.loli.net/2020/03/19/J1qlUrQx9LiTFY2.png?size=large 2x" data-title="img" style="background: url(/images/loading.min.svg) no-repeat center;" onload="this.title=this.dataset.title;for(const i of ['style', 'data-title','onerror','onload']){this.removeAttribute(i);}this.dataset.lazyloaded='';" onerror="this.title=this.dataset.title;for(const i of ['style', 'data-title','onerror','onload']){this.removeAttribute(i);}"/></p>
<h3 id="服务描述" class="heading-element"><span>服务描述</span>
 <a href="#%e6%9c%8d%e5%8a%a1%e6%8f%8f%e8%bf%b0" class="heading-mark">
